content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1160 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 28, 1976

Mr. Robert Tellander
California State College,
Sonoma
615 Keokuk Street
Petaluma, California 94952

Dear Mr. Tellander:

This is in response to your letter of December 15
inquiring about the obligation of doctors who serve on
local district hospital boards to disqualify themselves
pursuant to the provisions of Proposition Nine.

Government Code §87100, which embodies the provisions
of the Proposition, provides:

No public official at any level of
state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision
in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.

This general prohibition requires a public official to
disqualify himself from making or participating in certain
decisions of the board or agency of which he is a member.
Government Code §87103, defining "financial interest",
specifically sets forth the appropriate bases for disqualification.

Similar disqualification provisions will be embodied in
a Conflict of Interest Code applicable to the local agency.
However, the disqualification provisions of the Political
Reform Act prevent neither the holding of certain interests
nor the service on a board or commission. Thus, even though

Mr. Robert Tellander
December 28, 1976                                                Page Two


a public official may have to disqualify himself or herself
from a significant portion of the decisions of the board, the
Act would not require the official to leave the board or dis-
pose of his or her assets.

     Thank you for your concern.  I trust the foregoing has
been responsive.  If you have specific questions regarding a
conflict of interest within city or county agencies, your
county counsel or appropriate city attorney should be able
to provide more specific responses than the Fair Political
Practices Commission.

     Thank you.

                                                 Sincerely,


                                                 Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                                 Conflicts of Interest Division


DLS:glb

CALIFORNIA STATE COLLEGE, SONOMA
1501 EAST COTATI AVENUE                                    ROHNERT PARK, CALIFORNIA 94928

DEPARTMENT OF SOCIOLOGY
707-664-2581

15 DECEMBER 1976

CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
BOX 3071
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

GENTLEMEN:

SUE HIRZ AND LETTER FORWARDED I HAD LEGAL MEMORANDA FOR BOARD-
ROOM CR. INVOLVING VOTING INSTRUCTIONS ON SERVE ON THE LOCAL HEALTH
DISTRICT HOSPITAL BOARD, ARE NOT IN CONFLICT OF INTEREST AS DEFINED
IN THE NEW REGULATIONS ESTABLISHED WITH THE PASSAGE OF PROPOSITION 9.

[illegible] THAT TOWN [illegible] [illegible] IN THE [illegible] TO PROTECT
JURISDICTION. TO MY KNOWLEDGE, HOWEVER, NEITHER THE NEWSPAPER NOR THE
HOSPITAL BOARD HAS REQUESTED A CLARIFICATION OF THE VOTING PARTICIPATION
OF DOCTORS ON THE DISTRICT HOSPITAL BOARD. I WOULD LIKE ONE.

AS A FACULTY MEMBER AT SONOMA STATE COLLEGE, I HAVE LIMITED DUTY THE
[illegible] A MEMBER'S SEAT IN ONE OF THE MOST IMPORTANT BOARD
OF TRUSTEES LEVEL, THEREFORE, I DO NOT VIEW THE MATTER AS PREJUDICIAL TO
DOCTORS IF THEY CANNOT SERVE AS VOTING MEMBERS OF THE BOARD. COULD YOU
CLARIFY THE ISSUE FOR ME?

SINCERELY,

[signature]

[illegible] STREET
SEBASTOPOL, CALIFORNIA [illegible]

THE CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY AND COLLEGES

embedding.qa_text: December 28, 1976 Mr. Robert Tellander California State College, Sonoma 615 Keokuk Street Petaluma, California 94952 Dear Mr. Tellander: This is in response to your letter of December 15 inquiring about the obligation of doctors who serve on local district hospital boards to disqualify themselves pursuant to the provisions of Proposition Nine. Government Code §87100, which embodies the provisions of the Proposition, provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. This general prohibition requires a public official to disqualify himself from making or participating in certain decisions of the board or agency of which he is a member. Government Code §87103, defining "financial interest", specifically sets forth the appropriate bases for disqualification. Similar disqualification provisions will be embodied in a Conflict of Interest Code applicable to the local agency. However, the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent neither the holding of certain interests nor the service on a board or commission. Thus, even though Mr. Robert Tellander December 28, 1976 a public official may have to disqualify himself or herself from a significant portion of the decisions of the board, the Act would not require the official to leave the board or dispose of his or her assets. Thank you for your concern. I trust the foregoing has been responsive. If you have specific questions regarding a conflict of interest within city or county agencies, your county counsel or appropriate city attorney should be able to provide more specific responses than the Fair Political Practices Commission. Thank you. Sincerely, [signature] Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief Conflicts of Interest Division DLS:g1b The letter is from the Department of Sociology at California State College, Sonoma, addressed to the California Fair Political Practices Commission. The writer expresses concern about the voting participation of doctors on the local hospital board and seeks clarification regarding their eligibility under Proposition 7, which was passed in 1976. The writer states that some time ago, a letter appeared in the local newspaper suggesting that doctors serving on the hospital board were not in conflict of interest as defined by new regulations issued with Proposition 7. However, neither the newspaper nor the hospital board has requested a clarification of the voting participation of doctors on the district hospital board. The writer, as a faculty member at Sonoma State College, views the matter as not prejudicial to doctors if they cannot serve as voting members of the board and requests clarification. Sincerely, [signature] Ralph Tellander
